title: My Favorite Supplement to Improve Focus & Performance

chunks.title: Dietary Changes and Experimentation
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses recent changes in their diet, noting a reduction in meat consumption and an increase in carbohydrates through juices and honey. They are experimenting with their macronutrient ratios and considering supplementing with creatine to maintain protein intake while diversifying their diet.

chunks.title: Benefits of Creatine Supplementation
chunks.summary: The speaker highlights the numerous benefits of creatine supplementation, including improvements in cognitive function and exercise performance. They explain that even individuals who consume meat may not obtain sufficient creatine and describe the positive effects they have experienced personally from supplementation.
chunks.transcript: it. I'm still getting around 1 gram of protein per pound of body weight, but more of that protein is coming from milk, and less of it is coming from meat. If you don't know about creatine, it's made in the human body from arginine and glycine and then methylated with something called SAM E. I will return to talking about SAM E later in this podcast, but we don't make enough of it to be optimal. chunks.title: Creatine and Kidney Health
chunks.summary: Addressing concerns about kidney health, the speaker clarifies that elevated creatinine levels due to creatine supplementation do not necessarily indicate kidney issues. They suggest monitoring creatinine levels with a focus on other markers for kidney function to ensure overall health.

chunks.title: Creatine Supplementation Protocols
chunks.summary: The speaker outlines various effective protocols for creatine supplementation, suggesting both short-term saturation and a gradual approach to increase levels in the body. They encourage individuals to consider the affordability and availability of quality creatine supplements.
chunks.transcript: I'm sure there's a lot of recovering vegans and vegetarians even listening to this right now. In terms of protocols for creatine supplementation, I think the literature supports a couple of different things. If you want to quickly saturate your muscles in your body with creatine for optimal performance across multiple areas of function, a protocol that is widely discussed is five grams of creatine four times a day for 5 to seven days. You can also do 3 to five grams of creatine for about 30 days to saturate your body with creatine in the stores. Now, that will cost you pennies per day. So, I think it's worth it for most people. There's lots of good brands out there that have certificates of analysis showing that they are pretty darn clean sources. So, creatine is interesting. It's something I've been experimenting with.
